title: Orbital Angular Momentum Textures and Currents in a Discrete Helix: Equilibrium and Linear Response
authors: Danny Cordova, Bertrand Berche, Ernesto Medina
abstract: Recently, nonequilibrium orbital angular momentum in low-dimensional systems has attracted renewed attention. Here we introduce a minimal three-orbital tight-binding model for a single helical chain and show that chirality alone generates a momentum-dependent orbital-angular-momentum texture through Slater--Koster hybridization in the local basis $(p_r,p_φ,p_z)$, without requiring atomic spin--orbit coupling. In the single-helix geometry, the radial orbital texture vanishes identically, while the azimuthal and longitudinal components remain finite and arise from the odd-in-momentum $(p_z,p_r)$ and $(p_r,p_φ)$ sectors. As a result, the equilibrium average orbital texture vanishes by parity, although persistent-like orbital angular momentum currents may still exist and imply chirality-dependent end magnetization in a finite helix. Under an applied longitudinal electric field, the system develops a finite orbital Edelstein response, whereas the projected longitudinal orbital-current conductivity vanishes in the linear regime by parity. When spin degrees of freedom are included, the orbital texture acts as a source of spin polarization through orbital-to-spin transduction. The resulting spin response is controlled by orbital overlap scales much larger than the bare relativistic spin--orbit scale, making it a stronger candidate for spin injection than the conventional spin Edelstein mechanism. These results identify chirality as the minimal microscopic ingredient for generating orbital angular momentum response in one-dimensional systems and support an orbital route to spin selectivity in chiral conductors.
summary: This work demonstrates that intrinsic chirality in a 1D helical structure is sufficient to generate orbital angular momentum textures using only orbital hybridization, bypassing the need for atomic spin-orbit coupling. The theory predicts a finite orbital Edelstein response and a robust orbital-to-spin transduction mechanism for generating spin polarization. This establishes chirality as a minimal ingredient for controlling spin selectivity in chiral conductors.
main_problem: The paper investigates the generation and control of orbital angular momentum (OAM) textures and currents in low-dimensional, chiral systems.
main_result: Chirality alone generates a momentum-dependent OAM texture without needing atomic spin-orbit coupling, leading to a finite orbital Edelstein response and a mechanism for spin polarization stronger than conventional SOC effects.
method: The study employs a minimal three-orbital tight-binding model solved using Bloch wavevector analysis to calculate expectation values of local angular momentum operators.
model_or_system: The system is a single helical chain modeled by a three-orbital tight-binding Hamiltonian using a local basis $\{p_r, p_\phi, p_z\}$. The hopping terms are derived from Slater-Koster hybridization rules based on the helical geometry.
key_observables: Orbital angular momentum texture components ($\langle L_r angle, \langle L_\phi angle, \langle L_z angle$), orbital Edelstein response ($\chi_L$), and induced spin polarization ($\delta S$).
important_parameters: Chirality ($\eta$), longitudinal electric field ($E_\parallel$), and the orbital overlap scales relative to the bare SOC scale.
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on a minimal three-orbital tight-binding approximation and uses linear response theory for the electric field application.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the helical model, the momentum-dependent orbital texture $\langle L_\phi angle(k)$ in the $	ext{pr}-	ext{pz}$ sector, and the accumulation of orbital angular momentum at the helix termination.
paper_structure: The paper systematically analyzes the OAM texture generation (equilibrium vs. linear response), details the orbital-to-spin transduction mechanism, and contrasts the resulting spin response with conventional SOC mechanisms.
why_interesting: The finding that chirality itself can generate robust OAM responses and mediate spin polarization via orbital overlap scales, bypassing the need for strong intrinsic SOC, is highly relevant for understanding spin-charge conversion in low-dimensional quantum materials.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (Color online). Model of the helix with the nearest neighbor sites rn and rn+1. The included orbitals per site are pr, pϕ, pz as depicted. The vector ˆdn joins two adjacent sites. The orientation chosen for the orbital corresponds to a single strand DNA like structure.

FIG. 2. Band structure of the reduced trz-only model. The azimuthal band Eϕ(k) (online black) remains fully de- coupled, while the pr −pz sector hybridizes through the odd- in-momentum coupling Yk, yielding the split branches E+(k) (online red) and E−(k) (online blue). The parameters used are reported in the appendix.

FIG. 3. Exact local orbital angular momentum textures for the fully hybridized 3 × 3 helical Hamiltonian. (a) Azimuthal texture ⟨Lϕ⟩generated primarily by the pr −pz coherence. (b) Longitudinal texture ⟨Lz⟩generated primarily by the pr −pϕ coherence. The textures corresponding to the low- est energy band E1 (online blue), the middle energy band E2 (online red), and the highest energy band E3 (online green) are shown. The texture for the radial angular moment is al- ways zero within the single helix model presented here.

FIG. 4. Orbital angular momentum textures given by Eq.63. for this case we only have a ⟨Lϕ⟩(k) due to the coherence of pz and pr orbitals. The length of the orbital angular momentum vector (online green) depends on k and is odd under k →−k.

FIG. 5. Azimuthal Orbital Angular Momentum Texture ⟨Lϕ⟩ generated by the (pz, pr) sector. The texture generated by E+ (online red) has the same shape as the texture generated by E−(online blue), but with the opposite sign.
